title: $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING
text: $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING Allow users to debug their own 3GL components using a 3GL debugger. $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING = TRUE | FALSE Defaults Assignment file: application assignment files Section: [SETTINGS] Default value: FALSE Description When $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING is set to TRUE , you can attach a 3GL debugger to an already running instance of Uniface to debug your own 3GL components. This helps in avoiding any interference to some of the operations Uniface performs. This applies both to 3GL components that run using the activate statement and those run using the perform statement. If your 3GL debugger starts Uniface, $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING will be set to TRUE automatically. Also, when $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING is set to TRUE, it disables decryption of settings encrypted by the Pathscrambler, so your assignment files must not have been generated by the Pathscrambler. This setting needs to be included in the assignment file of the application process that is actually running the 3GL component to be debugged. It should never be placed in the URouter assignment file, as URouter ignores it. Typically, it should not be added in the UServer assignment file either—unless that specific UServer is running the 3GL component. In such cases, the client application's assignment file must also include the setting. Note: You must use $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING setting in your development environments only, and not in a production environment. A deployed application should never have this setting in any of its assignment files. Example: $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING $ENABLE_USER_3GL_DEBUGGING = TRUE Copy Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
